1: The righteous man perishes and no one takes it to heart — faithful men are taken away and no one understands. The righteous man is taken away from the evil to come. Social obliviousness to the deaths of the godly is a recurring prophetic complaint: the community fails to read the departure of the righteous as a sign. The phrase taken away from the evil to come interprets the righteous person's early death as providential rescue — they are removed before the coming judgment arrives, a mercy misread as tragedy.
2: He enters into peace — they rest in their resting places, each one who walked uprightly. The righteous person's death as entry into shalom is an early formulation of afterlife hope: the upright dead are in a state of rest rather than the anxious unresolved condition of those who die under judgment. The resting places are not merely graves but a condition of settled covenantal security beyond death.
3: But you — come here, you children of the sorceress, offspring of the adulterer and the prostitute. The diatribe shifts abruptly to the syncretistic community. Sorceress, adulterer, and prostitute are not merely moral categories but describe the three dimensions of Israel's covenant apostasy: sorcery (divination, seeking knowledge outside YHWH), adultery (the covenant faithlessness toward YHWH as husband), and prostitution (hiring out covenant loyalty to foreign powers and their gods in exchange for political security).
4: Whom are you mocking? Against whom do you open your mouth wide and stick out your tongue? Are you not children of rebellion, the offspring of lies? Mouth-opening and tongue-extension were ANE gestures of contempt and defiance. The mocking target is implicitly YHWH and his prophets. The designation children of rebellion (yalde pesha) and offspring of lies frames the idolatrous community's identity as constituted by their apostasy — they are what they practice.
5: You who inflame yourselves among the trees, under every leafy tree — who slaughter children in the ravines, under the crevices of the rocks. The high-place fertility cult worship under trees was the standard description of Canaanite religious practice that Israel adopted (Deuteronomy 12:2; Hosea 4:13). Child sacrifice in the valley of Hinnom (Topheth) is the specific horror alluded to — burning children as offerings to Molech (2 Kings 23:10; Jeremiah 7:31). The rocky crevices as sacrifice sites suggest remote locations for hidden cult activity.
6: Your lot is among the smooth stones of the streambeds — they are your portion; you have poured out drink offerings to them and brought grain offerings. Should I be appeased by these things? The smooth stones of streambeds may represent the masseboth (standing stones) used in Canaanite shrine practice, or specific deity-stones associated with stream deities. Pouring libations and presenting grain offerings to rocks is the direct inversion of covenant worship: the correct offerings redirected to non-entities. YHWH's rhetorical question expects the answer: no.
7: On a high and lofty mountain you set your bed — you went up there to offer sacrifice. The bed on the high mountain combines the spatial location of the Baalist high places with the sexual imagery of covenant apostasy as adultery. Going up to offer sacrifice on elevated terrain was the standard Canaanite cultic practice that prophets consistently condemned as the alternative to YHWH's centralized worship.
8: Behind the door and the doorpost you placed your memorial — away from me you uncovered yourself, spread your bed wide, and struck a deal with them; you loved their bed and looked upon what was exposed. The doorpost memorial inverts the Passover sign (mezuzah) that YHWH commanded: instead of his name on the doorpost, an idol. The uncovering, bed-spreading, and deal-making sustain the adultery metaphor: covenant apostasy is a sexual relationship with the idols rather than YHWH.
9: You traveled to the king with oil and multiplied your fragrances — you sent your messengers far off, even down to Sheol. Traveling to the king with tribute oil was the standard ANE diplomatic protocol for vassal acknowledgment of a suzerain. Whether the king is a foreign power (Assyria, Babylon, Egypt) or the deity Molech, Israel has substituted a false suzerain for YHWH. The messengers sent even to Sheol — the underworld — suggests necromantic consultation: seeking guidance from the dead rather than YHWH (cf. Isaiah 8:19; 1 Sam 28).
10: You grew weary from your many journeys but you did not say, 'It is hopeless' — you found renewed strength, and so you were not weak. The tireless pursuit of idolatry despite the exhaustion it produces is the image of addiction: the spiritual energy invested in false worship renews itself because the idolater has found (false) vitality in it. This is different from the renewal of strength YHWH promises (Isaiah 40:31) — the idol worshiper's renewal is the self-sustaining energy of a destructive habit.
11: Whom did you fear and dread, that you lied and did not remember me or call it to mind? Was it not because I was silent and looked away that you have not feared me? YHWH's silence during the exile period was interpreted by the apostates as his permanent absence or impotence. The divine non-intervention that seemed to validate the idol-worship was actually YHWH's patience. His silence was mistaken for consent; his looking away was mistaken for defeat.
12: I will expose your righteousness and your deeds — but they will not benefit you. The ironic your righteousness (tzidkatekh) refers to the formal religious practice the community considers meritorious — their temple-cult performances and idol-worship — which YHWH will expose as the sham it is. The moral inventory of ANE legal proceedings exposed the defendant's deeds before the judge; here YHWH conducts that examination and finds the self-styled righteousness worthless.
13: When you cry out, let your collection of idols rescue you — the wind will carry them all off, a breath will sweep them away. But whoever takes refuge in me will possess the land and inherit my holy mountain. The wind-dispersal of idols is the anti-idol polemic's most devastating image: the gods you trusted cannot even maintain their own stability against a breeze. The pivot in the second half of the verse establishes the alternative: those who take refuge in YHWH receive the inheritance that the idol-worshipers will lose — land and holy mountain.
14: A voice will say: Build up, build up, prepare the way — clear every obstruction from the road for my people. The highway-preparation command recapitulates Isaiah 40:3-4 and Isaiah 62:10. The road-clearing for the returning exiles was the standard royal infrastructure task in the ANE: great kings cleared, leveled, and maintained roads for processions and military movements. YHWH's highway-preparation surpasses all royal road-building because it removes every obstacle for the covenant community's return.
15: For thus says the One who is high and lifted up, who inhabits eternity, whose name is Holy: I dwell in the high and holy place, and also with the one who has a contrite and humble spirit, to revive the spirit of the humble and to revive the heart of the contrite. The divine paradox of simultaneous transcendence and immanence: YHWH inhabits eternity in the highest cosmic space AND dwells with the crushed (daka) and lowly of spirit. The word contrite shares a root with the Servant's crushing in Isaiah 53:5 — YHWH specifically inhabits the space of the shattered.
16: For I will not contend forever, nor will I be angry forever — for the spirit would faint before me and the breath of life that I have created. Divine anger is self-limiting not because of its weakness but because of the fragility of its object. If YHWH's anger were permanent and total, the created spirits he has made would disintegrate: they cannot survive unrelenting divine wrath. The restraint of judgment is therefore an act of preservation consistent with YHWH's creator role.
17: Because of the iniquity of his greed I was angry; I struck him; I hid my face and was angry — but he kept going, backsliding along the way of his own heart. The hidden-face withdrawal of divine presence did not produce repentance but escalating apostasy. The pattern — discipline → continued sin — is the diagnosis of the exile community's spiritual condition. YHWH struck, hid his face, and the people simply continued on their own path: the discipline did not achieve its corrective purpose.
18: I have seen his ways, yet I will heal him — I will lead him and restore comfort to him and to those who mourn with him. Divine healing despite observed waywardness is the covenant mercy that transcends the principle of proportional response. YHWH sees the backsliding (Isaiah 57:17) and responds not with further punishment but with healing. The leading and comforting are shepherding actions: YHWH as the good shepherd who retrieves the wandering flock rather than abandoning them to their own path.
19: Creating the fruit of the lips — peace, peace, to the far and to the near, says YHWH, and I will heal them. YHWH creates speech — the fruit of the lips — as an act parallel to his creation of the physical world. The double shalom shalom (peace, peace) is the emphatic doubled blessing: comprehensive covenant wellbeing extended to both those near (the returned exiles) and those far (the diaspora still scattered). Ephesians 2:17 quotes this verse as fulfilled in Christ: he preached peace to those far away and peace to those who were near.
20: But the wicked are like the churning sea — it cannot be still, and its waters stir up mire and mud. The chaos-sea imagery recurs from Isaiah 51:9-10 but now applied to the wicked within the community: they have the inner agitation of the restless deep, unable to achieve the shalom promised to the humble and contrite. The churning that produces mire and mud is the moral pollution generated by the absence of covenant peace.
21: There is no peace, says my God, for the wicked. The refrain appears at 48:22 and here at Isaiah 57:21, forming a deliberate structural bracket around the central section of chapters 49–57. The no peace for the wicked declaration is not a threat of future punishment but a present diagnosis: the covenantal condition of shalom is structurally unavailable to those who reject the covenant. Peace is not a reward withheld; it is the natural consequence of covenant relationship that the wicked have excluded themselves from.
